coredata.dc:title: Improving adsorption and visible light photocatalytic performance of TiO2 via synergistic effect of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and reduced mildly oxidized graphene oxide for cationic dye pollutants removal
coredata.dc:creator: Tao, Yang
Mou, Zhigang
Lei, Weining
Li, Yuxuan
Shangguan, Li
Zhu, Wen
Gong, Jie
Sun, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a new type of ternary composite for efficient removal of cationic dye pollutants, P25/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced mildly oxidized graphene oxide (P25/NGQDs/rmGO) was successfully prepared by a two-step hydrothermal process. P25/NGQDs/rmGO exhibited outstanding removal performance towards methylene blue (MB), crystal violet (CV) and Basic Red 46 (BR46) under visible light or solar light irradiation owing to the excellent adsorption-photocatalytic property resulting from the synergistic effect of NGQDs and rmGO. The negatively charged NGQDs significantly improved the adsorption capacity for positively charged dye based on the adsorption experiment results. The investigations of adsorption kinetics and isotherm for MB demonstrated that the adsorption process followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and Langmuir adsorption isotherm, respectively. Thermodynamics analysis indicated that the adsorption process was exothermic and spontaneous. Furthermore, NGQDs and rmGO could both extend photoresponse range and enhance charge separation and transportation based on UV–vis DRS, PL spectra, transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra results. The optimized P25/NGQDs/rmGO composite obtained the removal efficiencies of 96.6 %, 100 % and 82.4 % for MB, CV and BR46, respectively, after 30-min adsorption and 150-min photodegradation under visible light irradiation. In addition, solar light irradiation was more favorable for the removal of these cationic dye pollutants. The high adsorption and photocatalytic degradation performance of P25/NGQDs/rmGO make it an excellent candidate for removing cationic dye pollutants from the industrial effluents. This study also provided a new idea on the roles of NGQDs and rmGO in adsorption-photocatalysis systems and promoted the practical application of TiO2-based composites in environmental field.

head.ce:title: Improving adsorption and visible light photocatalytic performance of TiO2 via synergistic effect of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and reduced mildly oxidized graphene oxide for cationic dye pollutants removal

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute of Advanced Functional Materials for Energy, Jiangsu University of Technology, Changzhou, 213001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Mechanical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Technology, Changzhou, 213001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a new type of ternary composite for efficient removal of cationic dye pollutants, P25/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced mildly oxidized graphene oxide (P25/NGQDs/rmGO) was successfully prepared by a two-step hydrothermal process. P25/NGQDs/rmGO exhibited outstanding removal performance towards methylene blue (MB), crystal violet (CV) and Basic Red 46 (BR46) under visible light or solar light irradiation owing to the excellent adsorption-photocatalytic property resulting from the synergistic effect of NGQDs and rmGO. The negatively charged NGQDs significantly improved the adsorption capacity for positively charged dye based on the adsorption experiment results. The investigations of adsorption kinetics and isotherm for MB demonstrated that the adsorption process followed the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and Langmuir adsorption isotherm, respectively. Thermodynamics analysis indicated that the adsorption process was exothermic and spontaneous. Furthermore, NGQDs and rmGO could both extend photoresponse range and enhance charge separation and transportation based on UV–vis DRS, PL spectra, transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra results. The optimized P25/NGQDs/rmGO composite obtained the removal efficiencies of 96.6 %, 100 % and 82.4 % for MB, CV and BR46, respectively, after 30-min adsorption and 150-min photodegradation under visible light irradiation. In addition, solar light irradiation was more favorable for the removal of these cationic dye pollutants. The high adsorption and photocatalytic degradation performance of P25/NGQDs/rmGO make it an excellent candidate for removing cationic dye pollutants from the industrial effluents. This study also provided a new idea on the roles of NGQDs and rmGO in adsorption-photocatalysis systems and promoted the practical application of TiO2-based composites in environmental field.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic removal of dye pollutants into non-toxic products has attracted much attention owing to its potential to purify wastewater that is discharged from dye manufacturing industries [1]. In the last decade, photocatalytic oxidation using semiconductors as catalysts has become one of the most promising approaches for removing dyes. Among semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is the most widely used inorganic photocatalyst due to its fairly good photoactivity, long-term chemical stability, nontoxicity, low cost and commercial availability [2–4]. However, pure TiO2 suffers from a large bandgap (3.2 eV for anatase and 3.0 eV for rutile), which allows it to absorb only the ultraviolet light (only 3∼5 % of the solar energy) [5]. It also exhibits high recombination rate of photogenerated electron and hole pairs resulting in the annihilation of each other, thus affecting photocatalytic activity of TiO2 [6]. Therefore, for practical use, the development of TiO2-based photocatalysts with high photocatalytic efficiency using visible light is extremely important.

Aiming at the above mentioned problems, some researchers propose to couple TiO2 with carbon materials to improve TiO2 photocatalytic activity. These carbon materials, including graphene [7,8], carbon nanotubes [9], fullerenes [10], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [11,12] and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [13–15], can extend excitation wavelength and suppress recombination of charge carriers of TiO2 with good electrical conductivity. Meanwhile, these carbon materials with high adsorption capability by π–π staking interaction can quickly adsorb dyes and hence increase the chances for the dyes and their intermediates to contact with TiO2 for improving the photocatalysts' degradability [16,17].

Graphene as one of the carbon-based nanomaterials is extensively used for adsorption and photocatalytic degradation due to its high thermal conductivity, excellent electron mobility, optical transparency and good chemical stability. However, pristine graphene is an inert material which possesses hydrophobic nature and poor dissolution in water, restraining its utilization in wet chemistry synthesis of graphene-based hybrids. Since graphene oxide (GO) with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups shows the attractive properties of facile preparation, good water solubility and affinity for the surface of semiconductor [18], it has been extensively used as a starting precursor during the production of graphene-based semiconductor photocatalysts [19,20]. However, the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in GO reduces its electrical conductivity, which is undesirable for photocatalytic reactions. Although majority of oxygen-containing functional groups can be removed in the subsequent reduction process, the structural defects are unavoidably left on the reduced graphene oxide (rGO), leading to the decrease of electrical conductivity of rGO [21,22]. So the balance between relatively low defect and hydrophilic property of GO should be considered in the preparation of graphene-based semiconductor composite photocatalysts [23–25].

GQDs, single or few layer graphene with a thickness of only a few nanometers, have been explored in modification of photocatalysts for their efficient optical absorption, unique photoluminescence and rapid photoinduced electron transfer. The nitrogen atom has been widely used for chemical doping of carbon nanomaterials [26]. Nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGQDs) have shown great potential for excellent photocatalytic degradability of dye pollutants over NGQDs/TiO2 composite [27]. Meanwhile, NGQDs usually contain abundant negatively charged oxygen-containing functional groups at the edge of surface [28], allowing electrostatic interaction with positively charged dye pollutants apart from π–π staking interaction, thus endowing NGQDs with excellent adsorption performance for cationic dye pollutants [29]. Actually, a good surface adsorption capability of the photocatalyst is a prerequisite for effective photocatalysis, as the photocatalytic degradation reactions occur predominantly on the surface.

According to above analysis, it is reasonable to combine TiO2 with NGQDs and low-defect graphene to achieve high adsorption-photocatalysis bi-function for efficient removal of cationic dyes by synergistic effect of NGQDs and low-defect graphene. The low-defect graphene with good electrical conductivity can suppress recombination of charge carriers of TiO2; NGQDs can expand optical absorption and improve cationic dye pollutants adsorption performance through electrostatic interaction. Similar studies were also reported for combining the GQDs/CQDs and rGO with different semiconductor photocatalysts to achieve the excellent photodegradation performance [30–33]. However, the specific contributions of NGQDs especially for the absorption of cationic dye pollutants, and low-defect graphene for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity in semiconductor photocatalytic system have never been quantified.

In this work, we prepared mildly oxidized graphene oxide (mGO) by modified improved Hummers method, which has good water solubility, low initial oxygen content with low-defect density. Then a ternary P25/NGQDs/reduced mildly oxidized graphene oxide (P25/NGQDs/rmGO) composite was prepared via a two-step hydrothermal process, in which the firstly formed P25/NGQDs were loaded on mGO by the secondary hydrothermal reaction for forming P25/NGQDs/rmGO photocatalyst. Adsorption kinetics, isotherms and thermodynamic studies for the adsorption of cationic dye methylene blue (MB) onto P25/NGQDs/rmGO composite were performed. Under visible-light illumination, the prepared P25/NGQDs/rmGO showed significantly enhanced removal efficiencies of cationic dye pollutants such as MB, crystal violet (CV) and Basic Red 46 (BR46) compared with pure P25, P25/rmGO and P25/NGQDs, which was attributed to the excellent adsorption and photocatalytic activity for cationic dye pollutants. Additionally, the roles of NGQDs and rmGO in adsorption and photocatalysis were further evaluated. The reasonable adsorption and photocatalytic degradation mechanisms were also proposed.

For synthesis of mildly oxidized graphene oxide (mGO), the Hummers method was modified by lowering the KMnO4 amounts from 3 to 2 wt equivalent of graphite powder and the oxidation temperature from 35 to 25 ℃. In addition, the 2nd step oxidation at 98 ℃ was omitted. The details are described as follows: Natural graphite powder (2.0 g) was added slowly into a round-bottom flask containing 46 ml of 98 % H2SO4 in an ice water bath and kept the temperature below 5 ℃ with stirring. Next, KMnO4 (4.0 g) was gradually added under the same conditions. The mixture was then placed in a water bath at 25 ℃ and stirred for 2 h. After the reaction had reached the oxidation time, the mixture was poured into 400 mL water, and the solution was stirred for another 15 min followed by the addition of 20 mL H2O2 (30 %). Next, they were subjected to aging overnight. After the top solution was decanted, the mixture was filtered, then washed with 5 % HCl aqueous solution and water. The washed samples were finally dried in a furnace at 50 ℃ for 24 h under air. The resulting solid was dispersed in water by ultrasonication for 1 h to make an aqueous dispersion. The obtained dispersion was then centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 30 min to remove the unexfoliated graphite to get a black-brown mGO suspension. For comparison, highly oxidized graphene oxide (hGO) was synthesized by the Hummers method with a modification of removing NaNO3 from the reaction formula [34].

The P25/NGQDs composites were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.672 g citric acid and 0.576 g urea were dissolved in distilled H2O (120 mL), and 0.8 g of P25 was added to the obtained solution and stirred for 2 h to get a homogeneous suspension. The suspension was then transferred into a 200 mL Teflon- lined autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the powders were collected by filtration, rinsed by deionized water and ethanol thoroughly. the resulting product was dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h. The obtained sample was denoted as P25/NGQDs-1. For comparison, following the same procedure as above except changing the amount of citric acid and urea with 1.344 g citric acid and 1.152 g urea; 2.016 g citric acid and 1.728 g urea, the obtained products were labeled as P25/NGQDs-2, P25/NGQDs-3, respectively.

A hydrothermal method was used again to prepare the P25/NGQDs/rmGO composites. Taking P25/NGQDs-2 for precursor as a typical example, a calculated amount (to obtain concentrations of 0.2, 0.5 and 1.0 wt% of mGO) of mGO suspension (0.1 mg mL−1) was diluted with deionized water (20 mL) and ethanol (10 mL) by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h, and 0.2 g of P25/NGQDs-2 was added to the mGO suspension, which was continuously stirred for 2 h. The dispersion was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and the hydrothermal process was performed at 120 °C for 3 h. Then, the obtained composites were centrifuged, rinsed with deionized water, and dried at 60 °C. The weight ratios (mGO: P25/NGQDs-2) of 0.2, 0.5 and 1.0 %, denoted as P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.2 %, P25/NGQDs/rmGO-0.5 % and P25/NGQDs/rmGO-1.0 %, respectively.

For comparison, P25/rmGO-0.5 % composite was prepared using the similar approach as P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % composite except that P25 instead of P25/NGQDs-2 was used. P25/rhGO-0.5 % was also prepared by a similar method as P25/rmGO-0.5 % composite except that an equal quantity of hGO was used instead of mGO.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100 instrument at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Cu Kα radiation were measured on a Philips diffractometer. The scanning rate is 1° min−1 in the 2θ range from 10 to 80° X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed by an AXIS Ultra DLD system (Kratos Analytical Inc.) equipped with monochromatic Al Kα radiation. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of samples were calculated by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms using a quantachrome QUDRASORB SI surface area and porosity analyzer. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted with a DSC 404F3 differential scanning calorimeter (NETZSCH, Germany) at a temperature scan rate of 10 °C min−1 in nitrogen. The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 spectrometer. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were obtained on a Hitachi UV-3010 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as a reference. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS920 fluorospectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

The transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were measured on a CHI 760E potentiostat/galvanostat electrochemical analyser by a typical three-electrode configuration system consisting of an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and the prepared samples as the working electrodes. The electrolyte was a 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. Transient photocurrent responses of different samples were carried out with bias potential of 50 mV under a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter. The EIS were measured under the perturbation signal of 5 mV over the frequency range from 1 to 100 MHz. The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 5 mg sample powder was thoroughly mixed with 1.0 mL ethanol and 0.5 mL Nafion (0.02 wt%) by ultrasonication to form a slurry, which was then applied evenly onto 1 cm2 of conducting indium tin oxide (ITO) glass substrate to form a film with thickness around 10 µm. After air drying, the working electrodes were further calcined at 120 °C for 2 h.

Adsorption capabilities of samples were evaluated through the adsorption of cationic MB as a model dye. Typically, 50 mg sample was added into 100 mL MB aqueous solution with certain initial concentrations. After continuously stirring in dark, 3 mL suspension was taken out and then filtered a Millipore filter (0.45 µm) at certain time intervals. The concentration of MB was detected by recording variations of the maximum absorption band (665 nm) in the UV–vis spectra of MB using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, Cary-100). In the kinetic study, the initial concentration of MB was 10 mg L−1 and temperature was kept at 25 °C. Adsorption isotherm tests were performed with different MB concentrations ranging from 5∼40 mg L−1 at 25 °C. The effect of temperature on adsorption was carried out at temperatures from 25∼55 °C with MB concentration at 10 mg L−1. To further investigate the influence of pH values on the adsorption of MB, the initial pH values of MB solution were adjusted by HCl (0.2 M) or NaOH (0.2 M). The amounts of adsorbed MB at time t (qt, mg g−1) and at equilibrium (qe, mg g−1) were calculated by the formula:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity test under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic reaction was conducted in a top-irradiation quartz vessel connected to a circulating water system to maintain at room temperature. A 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter (irradiation intensity: ∼96 mW cm−2) was used as a visible light resource. In a typical process, 100 mL cationic dye aqueous solution (MB, CV and BR46, 10 mg L−1) and 50 mg photocatalyst were added to a quartz vessel and the suspension was stirred in the dark for 30 min to ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium. At given time intervals of visible light illumination, the residual concentration of dye was measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at its maximum absorption wavelength. The removal efficiency was determined by calculating (1-C/C0)×100 %, where C is the detected concentration of dye at different time, while C0 represents the initial concentration of dye in the solution.

To further investigate the active species (•O2 −, •OH, and h +) of dye degradation, different sacrificial agents were added into the reaction solution to remove the reactive species. In this work, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, 10 mM), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 10 mM) and bubbling N2 were employed as the scavengers for hole (h +), hydroxyl free radical (•OH) and superoxide anion radical (•O2 −), respectively.

The structure of mGO was systematically characterized by several techniques. For comparison, a hGO sample was also synthesized via modified Hummers’ method [34]. The UV–vis spectra of mGO and hGO aqueous dispersions are shown in Fig. 1a . A main absorption peak at 236 nm and a shoulder peak at ∼300 nm are observed for mGO, which can be attributed to the π–π* transition of CC bond and the n–π* transition of CO bond, respectively. Compared with the main peak of at 230 nm for the spectrum of hGO, a 6 nm red-shift for mGO was observed. The spectral result reflects that mGO sheets have larger π-conjugated domains than hGO [22].

XRD analysis was used to characterize the crystalline nature and phase purity of the as-synthesized GO with different oxidation degrees. The XRD patterns of mGO and hGO are shown in Fig. 1b. As a reference, the XRD pattern of the pristine graphite is also shown. The XRD pattern of the graphite shows a diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.3° corresponding to an interlayer spacing of about 0.34 nm [35]. For mGO, a peak at 26.4° can be assigned to graphite, which is attributed to the graphite-like stacking of mGO sheets because of their large conjugated domains. While the peak become broadened compared with that of pristine graphite due to mild oxidation of graphite [35]. In addition, an another peak at 10.6° can be attributed to the (001) plane of the hexagonal crystalline structure of GO [36]. For hGO, apart from a characteristic (001) reflection peak of GO (2θ = 10.4°), there is no peak corresponding to graphite because of high oxidation. The presence of peaks at 26.4° and 10.6° strongly support the case of mild oxidation of mGO. In addition, the (001) reflection peak of hGO was shifted towards lower 2θ compared with mGO, suggesting the increase of the interlayer spacing. The increasing interlayer spacing of the hGO suggests that higher content of oxygen-containing groups were attached to the graphite lattice than that of mGO.

Fig. 1c presents the XPS survey spectra of mGO and hGO samples. Only oxygen and carbon are observed in both samples. The C/O atomic ratios measured by XPS survey spectra were calculated to be 2.59 and 2.40 for mGO and hGO, respectively. The result suggests that mGO was mildly oxidized. The high resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of mGO shows two types of carbon bonds: CC/CC (284.7 eV), COC (286.8 eV) (Fig. 1d). However, the peak intensity at 286.8 eV was obviously weaker than that of hGO. Moreover, a new peak located at 288.5 eV ascribed to COOH in the C 1s XPS spectrum of hGO was absent in that of mGO. These results further indicate that mGO has fewer oxygen-containing groups than that of hGO [22]. Thus, the mGO can be used as a precursor to prepare highly conductive, low-defect graphene through reduction, which was beneficial to charge transfer in photocatalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology characterization of as-prepared catalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The pure NGQDs could be prepared by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as precursors reported by Qu et al. [37]. The FTIR spectrum of NGQDs is shown in Fig. S1. The peaks around 3430 and 3180 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH [38]. Asymmetric stretching vibration of COO− is observed at 1580 cm−1 [39]. The peaks at 1400 and 1190 cm−1 are due to the presence of CN and COC, respectively [37,40]. The results indicate NGQDs possess a large number of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. In this work, the P25/NGQDs composites were also prepared by one step hydrothermal method using P25, citric acid and urea as precursors based on our previous similar report [41]. Subsequently, the secondary hydrothermal reaction was carried out using P25/NGQDs and mGO as precursors for forming P25/NGQDs/rmGO photocatalysts.

The morphology of the as-prepared P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % composite was investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2a , P25/NGQDs is well dispersed on the rmGO layers. The morphology of P25/NGQDs is spherical particles with average diameter around 30 nm. The transparent section is the monolayer graphene (Fig. 2b). Moreover, HRTEM image shows that NGQDs with the diameter about 5∼10 nm are uniformly distributed on the P25 surface (Fig. 2c), indicating that NGQDs have been successfully coupled with P25 by one step hydrothermal process. The lattice spacing of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % were determined to be 0.24 and 0.35 nm from the HRTEM image in Fig. 2d, corresponding to the (1120) crystal plane of NGQDs [41] and the (101) lattice plane of anatase [42], respectively. Therefore, the above results reveal that NGQDs and rmGO are successfully incorporated with P25 by a two-step hydrothermal process.

The TGA was conducted to estimate the amount of NGQDs in the P25/NGQDs-2 sample. As shown in Fig. 2e, the initial weight loss from room temperature to 150 °C can be due to the removal of physically adsorbed water. This process has also been reported in the literature [43,44]. Although samples in this study were dried at 60 °C for 24 h prior to testing, a small amount of moisture was reabsorbed after samples were taken out of the desiccator. TGA of pure P25 shows a 2.7 wt% below 150 °C. TGA of P25/NGQDs-2 shows a 3.0 wt% below 150 °C. For pure P25 sample, the 2.0 wt% weight loss between 150 °C and 600 °C is due to the weight loss of chemically bound water (OH groups) [43]. For the P25/NGQDs-2 sample, the 3.6 wt% weight loss between 150 °C and 600 °C is due to both OH groups and the slow combustion of NGQDs [12,45]. If the physically adsorbed and chemically bound water weight percentage is discounted in the TGA analysis, the amount of NGQDs loading is about 1.7 % for P25/NGQDs-2 sample.

The XRD patterns of P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are shown in Fig. 2f. All samples show sharp and intense reflections attributed to anatase and rutile. However, the typical XRD reflections for carbon were not observed in P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % samples, because of the low loadings of NGQDs/rmGO and also their low crystallinity compared with that of P25.

Fig. 3a displays the XPS survey spectra of P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % composites. These samples contain Ti, O, C and a small amount of N with the binding energies corresponding to Ti 2p, O 1s, C 1s and N 1s. The high resolution C 1s XPS spectra of P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are shown in Fig. 3b. Both samples exist in three peaks based on the peak fitting. The peaks at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.6 eV are assigned to the CC/CC, C–O/CNHx and COOH/N = CN2 bonds, respectively [46–48]. The peaks at 286.2 and 288.6 eV of P25/NGQDs-2 mainly originated from the oxygen-containing functional groups on NGQDs, indicating the incorporation of NGQDs into the P25. Moreover, the peak at 286.2 eV of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % is weaker than that of P25/NGQDs-2, meanwhile comparing the result of C 1s of mGO (see Fig. 1d), we can conclude that mGO is reduced to rmGO with simultaneous partial reduction of NGQDs by the solvothermal treatment.

The high resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p are shown in Fig. 3c. For P25/NGQDs-2, the binding energies of Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 of Ti4+ chemical states are positioned at 458.9 and 464.6 eV, respectively. Compared to P25/NGQDs-2, the Ti2p peaks are slightly shifted negatively to 458.6 and 464.3 eV after the introduction of rmGO. Similarly, the binding energies of O 1s corresponding to Ti-O and OCOH bonds show a little negative shift for P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % compared to P25/NGQDs-2 (Fig. 3d), meaning the Ti and O in P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % composite get electrons partially [49]. The above results reveal the strong interactions between Ti/O and C atoms from rmGO. The close interaction will lead to the efficient separation of the photo-induced carriers and contribute to the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency. The high-resolution N 1s spectra of P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are shown in Fig. S2. The results imply that NGQDs are present in P25/NGQDs and P25/NGQDs/rmGO composites. In addition, the binding energies of N 1s in P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % also have a negative shift in comparison with that of P25/NGQDs-2, indicating the interaction between NGQDs and rmGO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical spectra and photogenerated charge properties
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % were analyzed by UV–vis DRS (Fig. 4a ). It is clearly seen that P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % exhibit broad visible absorption compared with pure P25, indicating that both NGQDs and rmGO can enhance the light harvesting capability [12,50]. Using the transformed Kubelka-Munk function shown in Fig. 4b, the band gap values of P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are estimated to be 3.12, 2.77 and 2.64 eV, respectively, suggesting that the modification with NGQDs and rmGO can narrow the band gap of P25 and expand the visible light response range.

PL analysis is frequently employed to reveal the rate of electron-hole recombination upon light irradiation, and weaker PL intensity usually represents lower recombination rate of electron-hole pairs [51]. Fig. 5a shows the PL spectra of samples with the excitation wavelength at 350 nm. All the samples exhibit broad PL signals in the wavelength range 390–600 nm and the spectra are similar in shape. This indicates that PL signals are originated from P25, which may be due to the small effect of low NGQDs/rmGO loading. A weak shoulder peak at 398 nm and a broad peak at 413 nm are attributed to band edge luminescence which is related to photoinduced electron-hole recombination in conduction and valance band of TiO2 [52]. In addition, there are four small peaks at 450, 468, 481, 492 nm observed in the wavelength range from 440 to 500 nm. These PL signals are due to excitonic PL, which mainly result from surface oxygen vacancies and defects of the TiO2 samples [53,54]. A 558 nm peak might be associated with transitions of electrons from the conduction band edge to deep trap holes owing to oxygen vacancies [52]. The intensities of the peaks at 398 and 413 nm which are detrimental to the transfer of photo-generated carriers have been greatly reduced with introduction of NGQDs and rmGO into P25, indicating that introduction of NGQDs/rmGO could effectively suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs because of efficient electron transfer from P25 to NGQDs/rmGO [30].

In order to further investigate the charge separation and transfer properties, the transient photocurrent measurements of P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % ITO electrodes were conducted under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5b, all the samples exhibit photocurrent responses in each light on-off cycle. Obviously, P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % ternary composite shows the highest photocurrent density, which is about 3 and 1.5 times higher than those of pure P25 and P25/NGQDs-2, respectively, indicating the more efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the ternary composite [55,56]. The introduction of NGQDs and rmGO into P25 could increase the formation of photogenerated carriers and reduce their recombination rate [57]. Meanwhile, the Nyquist plot of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % displays an apparently declined semicircle compared with that of pure P25 and P25/NGQDs-2 (Fig. 5c), indicating the lower charge transport resistance and improved charge transport capability [58,59]. All above results strongly confirm the introduction of NGQDs and rmGO by a two-step hydrothermal process can improve charge separation and transfer, suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the ternary composite of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %.

MB was used as a typical cationic pollutant model to investigate adsorption capability of samples. Adsorption is a physicochemical process that involves transfer of an adsorbate from the liquid phase to the adsorbent surface. The adsorption of MB on P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are shown in Fig. 6a . The adsorption capacities of all samples increase firstly and then remain almost unchanged with time. The adsorption equilibrium is achieved after about 30 min. The adsorbed amounts at equilibrium of MB for pure P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are 2.78, 13.03 and 10.67 mg g−1, respectively. The adsorption capacity of MB on P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % is 3.84 times higher than that of pure P25. The oxygen-containing functional groups of NGQDs have a significant impact on the MB adsorption. The adsorption capacity decline slightly for P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % compared with P25/NGQDs-2 maybe due to the partial reduction of NGQDs, which was confirmed by XPS result (see Fig. 3b).

In order to further explore MB adsorption behavior and mechanism onto P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % sample, adsorption kinetics and isotherm were studied. The pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order model are tested for adsorption of MB dye onto P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %, whose equations are given respectively below:
(3)

)

where qe and qt represent the amount of dye adsorbed at equilibrium and at a specific time t (mg g−1). k1 (min−1) and k2 (g mg−1 min−1) are the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order rate constant, respectively.

The values of all kinetic parameters are presented in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 6b and Table 1, it is found that the pseudo-second-order model fits quite better with the experimental data (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9985) than that pseudo-first-order model (Fig. S3). The calculated qe value by pseudo-second-order kinetic model basically conforms to the experimental data, whereas in the case of pseudo-first-order kinetic model, there is no compliance between experimental and calculated value (Table 1). Therefore it can be concluded that adsorption of MB dye follows pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The result agrees well with the previous studies about MB adsorption by adsorbents such as hydroxyl group enriched TiO2 nanotube [60], amino-functionalized magnetic multiwall carbon nanotubes [61], and Zeolithe [62].

The adsorption isotherm is also essential to investigate the adsorption capability of adsorbent and interaction between the adsorbate and adsorbent. In this study, the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption models are selected to analyze the adsorption isotherms of MB onto P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % composite. The Langmuir isotherm model presumes monolayer adsorption on homogeneous sites, whereas the Freundlich model assumes multilayer adsorption over heterogeneous sites. Their models are given as follows:
(5)

K L

where K L (L mg−1) and K F (mg1-n Ln g−1) are the Langmuir and Freundlich constants, respectively; qm (mg g−1) is the Langmuir saturated absorption capacity; Ce (mg L−1) is the MB concentration in the solution at equilibrium; n is the adsorption intensity indicator in the Freundlich model.

The nonlinear fitting curves for adsorption isotherms are shown in Fig. 6c, with related isotherm constants of these two isotherm models listed in Table 2 . Compared to Freundlich model, Langmuir model with higher correlation coefficients (R2 = 0.9821) is more suitable to represent the isotherm, implying that the adsorption of MB on P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % belongs to monolayer adsorption. The maximum saturated adsorption capacity of MB calculated through the Langmuir model is 19.1770 mg g−1. Fast adsorption rate and large adsorption capability ensure the potential of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % for the rapid and effective removal of MB from water.

The temperature is an important factor influencing the adsorption of dye to the surface of adsorbent. The adsorption experiments were carried out at four different temperatures (25, 35, 45 and 55 °C) with 100 mL of MB solution (10 mg L−1) and 50 mg of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %. The result demonstrates that the adsorption capacities of MB decrease with raising the temperature over the range (Fig. 7A ), suggesting lower temperature is more conducive to MB adsorption. In addition, the insight of the adsorption mechanism can be determined from the thermodynamic parameters including Gibbs free energy (ΔG°), the enthalpy (ΔH°) and the entropy (ΔS°), which can be calculated by using the following equations:
(7)

where T (K) is the adsorption temperature, R (8.314 J mol−1 K−1) is the ideal gas constant, and K L (L mol−1) is the Langmuir constant. The plot of lnK L versus 1/T is linear (Fig. 7B), the values of ΔH° and ΔS° are obtained from the slope and intercept according to Eq. (9). The calculated thermodynamic parameters are given in Table 3 . The result demonstrates that the calculated ΔG° values are negative in the studied temperature range, indicating that the adsorption of MB onto P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % is a spontaneous and favorable process [63]. The negative value of enthalpy change (ΔH°=−33.838 kJ mol−1) indicates that adsorption is exothermic in nature. In addition, the negative value of ΔS°(−17.426 J mol−1 K−1) suggests the decrease in the randomness at the solid-liquid interface during the adsorption process [61]. This is a reflection of the MB dye affinity to adsorbent surface at equilibrium.

The BET specific surface areas by nitrogen adsorption-desorption analysis were calculated to be 59.64, 59.27 and 62.91 m2 g−1 for pure P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %, respectively (Fig. S4). The pore volumes of pure P25, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are 0.365, 0.279 and 0.295 cm3 g−1, respectively. The introduction of NGQDs into P25 and rmGO into P25/NGQDs do not lead to the significant increase of BET surface area due to the low loadings of NGQDs/rmGO, so the surface area might not be a key factor for high adsorption capacity for MB. To further elucidate the adsorption mechanism of P25/NGQDs/rmGO, FTIR spectroscopy was used to study the change of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % before and after MB adsorption. As shown in Fig. 7C, the FTIR spectrum of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % features a broad absorption band at 3390 cm−1, corresponding to the tensile vibrations of the OH bonds [64]. The prominent absorption peaks at around 1633, 1385 and 1055 cm−1 represent the OH stretching vibration, the OH bending vibration and the stretching vibrations of CO in carboxylic groups, respectively [65,66], indicating that P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % possesses abundant oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl groups and carboxylic groups. After MB adsorption, the intensities the OH band decreased, and the CO band disappeared, indicating hydroxyl groups and carboxylic groups on the adsorbent were involved in the adsorption of MB probably due to the electrostatic interaction between them [64,65,67]. Pure MB dye shows the characteristic peaks at 1586, 1310 and 1132 cm−1, which can be assigned to CN stretching band and CN bending vibration on the benzene ring for MB [68]. However, these characteristic peaks of MB are shifted to 1600, 1333 and 1152 cm−1, respectively, for P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % after MB adsorption. The bathochromic shifts further indicate that MB is absorbed on the surface of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % successfully with the strong interactions between them [69]. Apart from electrostatic interaction, MB is a planar molecule and therefore can be easily adsorbed by NGQDs/rmGO by π-π stacking interaction between the aromatic backbone of the dye and hexagonal skeleton of NGQDs/rmGO.

The pH value of dye solution strongly influences the surface charge of adsorbent, which directly affects adsorption behavior of dye. The effect of initial pH value was studied for MB adsorption by P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % in a range of 2.01–10.10 (Fig. 7D). Natural pH of MB solution without any pH adjustment is about 5.85. The amounts of MB adsorbed are found to increase from 3.07 to 10.67 mg g−1 with increasing pH values from 2.01 to 5.85, and then reach a plateau from 5.85 to 7.96. The adsorbed amounts increase again from 10.88 to 13.63 mg g−1 with increasing pH values from 7.96 to 10.10. The P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % contains a significant amount of oxygen-containing functional groups due to the presence of NGQDs, which can provide abundant adsorption sites for MB based on FTIR result of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % before and after MB adsorption (Fig. 7C). At low pH values, the adsorption capacity of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % for MB is distinctly restrained by excess hydrogen ions which can compete with MB cationic molecules for adsorption sites [65]. The decrease of available adsorption sites for MB molecules results in the low adsorption capacity. When pH values increase, the adsorption sites are released by the hydrogen ions, leading to more negative charges of the catalyst surface, which can enhance the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged MB and the negatively charged catalyst. The enhanced electrostatic attractive force results in higher adsorption amount of MB dye molecules from water under high pH values [70]. Based on the above FT-IR spectroscopy and the effect of pH value for MB adsorption, the electrostatic and π-π stacking interactions between P25/NGQDs/rmGO and MB can be responsible for the high adsorption ability of P25/NGQDs/rmGO. Since adsorption amounts by π-π stacking interaction increase along with the increasing of NGQDs/rmGO ratio [42], the electrostatic interaction is of primary importance due to the low loadings of NGQDs and rmGO for P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % sample.

Based on the above results of adsorption, the removal performances of MB by different samples under visible light irradiation were studied. Before the photocatalytic experiments, the suspensions were magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min. As shown in Fig. 8a , during the dark period, the photocatalysts show different adsorption abilities for MB. The adsorption ratio of the pure P25 is only ∼13 %. With the introduction of rhGO/rmGO into P25, the adsorption ratios of P25/rmGO-0.5 % and P25/rhGO-0.5 % increase, but the increase is not significant due to the low loadings of rhGO/rmGO. However, the adsorption capacities improve dramatically for P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % with ∼64 % and ∼53 % adsorption ratio, respectively.

For the photodegradation, the MB concentration presents a negligible change without photocatalyst under visible-light irradiation for 150 min, indicating that MB itself is stable and difficult to be photolysis. After 30-min adsorption and 150-min photodegradation under visible light, the pure P25 shows poor removal efficiency, and only 47.2 % of MB is removed. P25/rmGO-0.5 % shows relatively higher removal efficiency than that of P25/rhGO-0.5 % because rmGO has lower defect for mild oxidization, thus more conducive to the enhancement of photoinduced electron transfer [71]. The highest removal efficiency, 96.6 % of MB, is achieved by P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % composite. Although P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % shows relatively lower adsorption capacity than that of P25/NGQDs-2, the introduction of rmGO into P25/NGQDs-2 can further improve charge separation and transfer, resulting in the higher removal efficiency of MB. Fig. S5 shows the variation of UV–Vis absorption spectra of MB degradation over P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % under visible light illumination, in which the intensities of characteristic absorption peak of MB at 665 nm decrease with increasing the irradiation time. The reaction kinetic process of MB degradation was evaluated by a pseudo-first order model. The pseudo-first order kinetic equation is described using the following formula:
(10)
− ln
( C /
C e
)
= kt
where k refers to the apparent first-order rate constant (min−1), Ce is the absorbed equilibrium MB concentration (deducting the amount of MB adsorption), C is the MB concentration at each irradiation time t. The rate constant k can be determined from the slope of linear plot (Fig. 8b). k of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % is 0.0163 min−1, which is approximately 4.53, 3.40, 2.06 and 2.96 times higher than that of P25 (0.0036 min−1), P25/rhGO-0.5 % (0.0048 min−1), P25/rmGO-0.5 % (0.0079 min−1) and P25/NGQDs-2 (0.0055 min−1). The excellent removal of MB can be ascribed to high adsorption ability and improved photocatalytic activity due to the co-introduction of NGQDs and rmGO into P25. There is a synergistic effect between NGQDs and rmGO. The NGQDs significantly improve the adsorption capacity for MB based on the adsorption experiments results. Moreover, NGQDs and rmGO can both extend photoresponse range and enhance charge separation and transportation based on UV–vis DRS, PL spectra, transient photocurrent and EIS results.

We compared the performances of P25/NGQDs catalysts prepared with different NGQDs ratios (Fig. 8c). The result indicates that proper NGQDs on P25 are beneficial for the photocatalytic activity. The P25/NGQDs-2 catalyst exhibits the highest catalytic activity. The optimal mass ratio of NGQDs over P25/NGQDs-2 is 1.7 % based on above TGA analysis. The excess NGQDs may block the light absorption of P25. Similar result was also found in the literatures [41]. We also compared the performance of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO catalysts prepared with different rmGO ratios (Fig. 8d). The photocatalytic activities of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO are improved with the content of rmGO increasing from 0.2 % to 0.5 %, but further increase of rmGO content to 1.0 % leads to the decrease in photocatalytic activity owing to the shading effect of rmGO [72]. In general, the highest photocatalytic activity is obtained by P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %.

In order to confirm that P25/NGQDs/rmGO has also high adsorption ability and photocatalytic degradation activity for other cationic dye pollutants, another two typical cationic dye pollutants (CV and BR46) were chosen as target pollutants to test the adsorption-photocatalytic performance over P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %. As shown in Fig. 9a and b, during the dark period, P25/NGQDs-2 and P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % adsorbed much more CV and BR46 compared with pure P25. The result further indicates the NGQDs can improve the adsorption capacity for cationic dyes. For the photodegradation, the concentrations of CV and BR46 do not decrease in the absence of photocatalysts, indicating CV and BR46 are stable and barely degraded under visible-light illumination. The photocatalytic performances of different photocatalysts show that, in comparison with pure P25 and P25/NGQDs-2, P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % exhibits highest ability for CV and BR46 removal under visible-light irradiation. About ∼100 % of CV and 82.4 % of BR46 are eliminated after 30 min of adsorption and 150 min of photodegradation, respectively. Fig. 9c and d show the variations of UV–Vis absorption spectra of CV and BR46 degradation within the time interval over P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % under visible light illumination. The CV and BR46 dyes exhibit the maximum absorption peaks at ∼585 and ∼533 nm, respectively. With the increase of the irradiation time, the intensities of CV and BR46 absorbance continuously decrease, indicating the decrease of concentrations for CV and BR46.

The removal efficiencies of the cationic dye pollutants were further evaluated under solar light irradiation on a clear day of December 6–7, 2023 (between 10 AM-2 PM, Air temperature: 17–20 ℃) at Changzhou (east longitude: 119.917°; north latitude: 31.753°). The average irradiation intensity was ∼18 mW cm−2. From Fig. 10a -c, the P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % also exhibits higher removal efficiencies for MB, CV and BR46 than those of pure P25 and P25/NGQDs-2. The removal efficiencies of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % are ∼96.4 % for MB in 30 min, ∼99.8 % for CV in 60 min and ∼98.2 % for BR46 in 30 min under solar light irradiation. The original colors of MB solution (blue), CV solution (violet) and BR46 solution (red) were almost completely changed to colorless after adsorption and photocatalytic degradation under solar light (Fig. S6), indicating these cationic dye pollutants were nearly completely bleached. The apparent first-order rate constants k of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % for MB, CV and BR46 degradation are 0.081, 0.079 and 0.104 min−1, respectively, which are much higher than those of P25 and P25/NGQDs-2 for MB, CV and BR46, respectively (Fig. 10d).

To further investigate the removal efficiencies of cationic dye at the different pH in the water, herein, the effect of initial pH values range from 2.01 to 10.10 on the removal rate of MB was studied. As shown in Fig. 11a , the removal efficiencies of MB increase obviously from 18.8 % to 96.6 % with increasing pH from 2.01 to 5.85, and reach maximum at pH 5.85 (natural pH of MB solution). Then the removal efficiencies of MB decrease little with increasing pH from 5.85 to 10.10. The adsorption amounts of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % for MB gradually increase with increasing pH values from 2.01 to 5.85 (see Fig. 7D), the removal efficiencies increase correspondingly. However, the removal efficiencies decrease above pH 5.85 although the adsorption amounts of MB increase. The reason of this phenomenon may be due to the blocking the surface active sites of catalyst by excess adsorbed MB, resulting in light screening effect. The most of the visible light is absorbed by MB molecules before it reaches the catalyst surface. This reduces the photocatalytic degradation rate of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % due to the poor utilization of incident photons. This result was in accord with previous reports [7,73].

The stability of the ternary P25/NGQDs/rmGO composite is very important for practical application. The photocatalytic recycle experiment over P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % was conducted to examine the reusability and stability of the composite. The sample was filtered and washed with deionized water, then dried at 80 °C after each photocatalytic experiment. As depicted in Fig. 11b, the removal efficiency of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % for MB decreases slightly as the increase of the number of cycles in the same irradiation time, but it is still greater than 80 % after five cycles (14.5 h), which indicates that P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % photocatalyst has good photocatalytic stability and recyclability.

The XRD patterns and FTIR spectra of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % photocatalyst before and after degradation reaction are shown in Fig. 11c and Fig. S7. The XRD pattern of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % photocatalyst after five cycles has no obvious changes in structure compared with fresh P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %, indicating that P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % retains its crystal structure in the photocatalytic degradation process of MB. To further confirm the adsorbed MB dye is completely degraded after reaction and the stability of the catalyst, FTIR analysis was applied as shown in Fig. S7. For P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % after reaction, the absorption peaks at around 3390, 1633, 1385 and 1055 cm−1 are observed, which is similar with the original sample, indicating the catalyst retains almost stable. Interestingly, the peak at 1055 cm−1 reappeared after reaction which disappeared after MB adsorption. In addition, the characteristic peaks for MB at 1600 and 1333 cm−1 (which were bathochromic shifted after absorption) disappeared after reaction. Although the peak 1152 cm−1 still appeared, the intensity decreased obviously. These results indicate that adsorbed MB dye was almost completely degraded after reaction. Further, it was apparent that color of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % (grey white) was completely changed into blue color after adsorption of MB dye and the blue colored P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % was changed into original color after photocatalytic degradation of adsorbed MB dye (inset, Fig. S7). Together with the aforementioned results, it is concluded that P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % composite has a relatively high stability for repetitive use, which can be successfully regenerated by photocatalytic degradation of adsorbed dye molecules in water suspension.

Tests with different scavengers were used to determine the contribution of active species during MB dye photodegradation by P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % sample. The results are shown in Fig. 11d. Without scavengers, the removal efficiency of the photocatalyst is ∼96.6 % after 30-min adsorption and 150-min photodegradation under visible light, while it drops to 25.8 %, 76.1 % and 60.6 % upon adding IPA, EDTA-2Na and bubbling N2, respectively. The result implies that the •OH is the main reactive species in the degradation process, while •O2 − and h +also played a critical role.

Valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) was carried out to verify the valence band (VB) value of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 %. The VB value of the catalyst is estimated to be 2.50 eV vs. vacuum level based on VB-XPS analysis (Fig. S8). According to the equation (11):
(11)
E NHE
= Ф +
E
V L
− 4.44
where E NHE, Ф and E VL are represented potential of normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), electron work function of the instrument (4.26 eV) and potential of vacuum level, respectively. VB value of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % is 2.32 eV vs. NHE. According to the band gap (2.64 eV) obtained from UV–vis DRS (Fig. 4b), conduction band (CB) value of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % is −0.32 eV vs. NHE.

Based on the above results and analysis, the possible mechanisms of adsorption and photocatalytic degradation for MB by the P25/NGQDs/rmGO photocatalyst was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Since NGQDs contain a significant amount of oxygen-containing functional groups, the surface of the photocatalyst become negatively charged, making it easier to bind cationic dye MB through electrostatic interaction in solution. This explained the excellent adsorption performance of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % for MB. In addition, there is π-π stacking interaction between the NGQDs/rmGO and MB molecule, which can also aid in the binding of MB. Therefore, both electrostatic and π-π stacking interactions contribute to the rapid and efficient adsorption of MB onto the P25/NGQDs/rmGO.

The photocatalytic degradation reaction occurs between the P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % and absorbed MB dye molecules. The photocatalyst is excited under visible light irradiation, and then the photogenerated electrons on the CB of the photocatalyst can rapidly transfer to the NGQDs and rmGO, leading to the effective separation of electron-hole pairs. The CB potential of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % (−0.32 eV vs. NHE) is more negative than E 0(O2/•O2 −=−0.046 eV vs NHE), and the VB potential of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % (2.32 eV vs. NHE) is more positive than E 0(OH−/•OH=1.99 eV vs. NHE) [74]. Therefore, the electrons on the surface of NGQDs and rmGO can react with O2 to form •O2 −, meanwhile the holes could oxidize OH− to generate •OH directly. The generated reactive species (•O2 −, •OH, and h +) will then attack the MB molecules absorbed on the surface of photocatalyst, resulting in their degradation. Therefore, during the adsorption and photodegradation process of MB, the NGQDs and rmGO play an important role in enhancing the removal efficiency for MB. NGQDs possess considerable adsorption capability for cationic dye mainly due to strong electrostatic interaction, which was kinetically favorable for the surface chemical reaction. In addition, NGQDs and rmGO both extend sufficient light absorption, narrow the bandgap of P25 and suppress the recombination of charge carriers, which cause the improvement of removal efficiency for MB. The NGQDs and rmGO show the significance of the synergistic effect for adsorption and photocatalysis on cationic dyes removal.

In summary, a novel ternary P25/NGQDs/rmGO composite with high adsorption-photocatalysis was successfully prepared by a two-step hydrothermal process. The NGQDs significantly improved the adsorption capacity for cationic dye based on the adsorption experiments results. The adsorption behavior could be well fitted by pseudo-second-order kinetic model and Langmuir isotherm. The electrostatic and π-π stacking interactions between the catalyst and cationic dye account for the high adsorptive performance. The NGQDs and rmGO efficiently promoted visible-light absorption and charge separation and transportation, leading to high photodegradation activity. All of the above results remarkably improve the removal performances of P25/NGQDs-2/rmGO-0.5 % for cationic dye pollutants (MB, CV and BR46) under visible light or solar light irradiation. The reusability experiments suggested that the ternary material possessed good stability. The free radicals trapping experiments confirmed that •OH, •O2 − and h+ all play critical roles during the photocatalytic degradation process. This study opens a new possibility for NGQDs and rmGO in adsorption-photocatalytic removal of cationic dye pollutants.

Yang Tao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Zhigang Mou: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Weining Lei: Methodology. Yuxuan Li: Data curation. Li Shangguan: Data curation. Wen Zhu: Writing – review & editing. Jie Gong: Writing – review & editing. Jianhua Sun: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
